title: French toast
subtitle: Thick bread soaked in spiced egg custard and fried in butter.
excerpt: Thick slices of white bread soaked in beaten egg, milk, cinnamon, and vanilla, then fried in butter until golden and custardy inside. Served with maple syrup or icing sugar. A weekend breakfast that takes fifteen minutes.
sourceNotes: Pain perdu, meaning 'lost bread', appears in medieval French cookery as a method for using stale bread. The dish spread across Europe under various names: pain doré in Quebec, arme Ritter in Germany, torrijas in Spain, and eggy bread in British vernacular. The American name 'French toast' became standard in the United States from the nineteenth century and has since largely replaced the regional British names.

content.text: The thickness of the bread is what separates good French toast from thin eggy bread. Slices cut at least 2.5 cm thick give the custard time to soak through to the centre while the outside fries golden. Supermarket-sliced white bread is too thin; use a bloomer, a brioche loaf, or a thick-cut white.
Slightly stale bread is better than fresh. Fresh bread is too soft and can tear when soaking in the custard. Bread that is a day old holds together.
A shallow bowl for the custard, a 26 cm frying pan, and a spatula.
Beat {{eggs}} of eggs, {{whole-milk}} of milk, {{vanilla-extract}} of vanilla extract, and {{cinnamon-ground}} of ground cinnamon together in a wide shallow bowl.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a frying pan over a medium heat until foaming. Dip each bread slice into the custard and leave for 20–30 seconds per side, until well soaked but not falling apart. Fry for 2–3 minutes per side until deep golden. Work in two batches if needed, adding a little more butter for the second batch.
Serve immediately with maple syrup poured over, and a dusting of icing sugar if you like. French toast does not hold well — it goes soggy if left to sit.

items.symptom: Bread is soggy in the middle
items.cause: Too much soaking time, or fresh bread that absorbed too much custard.
items.fix: Slightly stale bread soaks in 20–30 seconds without going soggy. Fresh bread needs less time in the custard.

items.symptom: Toast is burnt outside but raw inside
items.fix: Medium heat only. The outside needs 2–3 minutes per side to develop colour; high heat browns it before the custard inside sets.

items.symptom: Not enough custard left to soak all the slices
items.cause: Thick slices absorb more than thin ones, or too many slices for the quantity.
items.fix: Add another beaten egg and a splash of milk to the bowl. Better to have too much custard than too little.

items.cause: Fresh bread or bread left too long in the custard.
items.fix: Use bread that is one to two days old. Lift the slice carefully with a spatula rather than tongs, which can tear it.

content.text: French toast is pain perdu under a different name, the same dish that has appeared across European cooking since at least the fourteenth century as a practical way to use stale bread. The spiced version with maple syrup is American in sensibility, brought into British domestic cooking through the influence of American breakfast culture in the 1990s and 2000s. The British name 'eggy bread' persists in some households, usually for a simpler version without the vanilla and maple syrup, closer to the medieval original.
